name: Linear Haskell IR
summary: GHC's Linear Haskell puts multiplicities on the function arrow — a %1 -> b is linear (consume exactly once), a %m -> b is multiplicity-polymorphic. The IR is an extended Core that carries multiplicities on every binding alongside its existing type and kind machinery.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Representation layout: GHC's Linear Haskell puts multiplicities on the function arrow — `a %1 -> b` is linear (consume exactly once), `a %m -> b` is multiplicity-polymorphic. The IR is an extended Core that carries multiplicities on every binding alongside its existing type and kind machinery.

sections.title: Implementation cost and ride-on-Core
sections.content:
Status (as of GHC 9.0+, available since 2021-02): the GHC implementation cost was small (~1,150 lines, per the paper) because multiplicities ride on top of Core's existing type infrastructure. Type-system semantics live in `data/linear-affine-types` and the language-level treatment in `data/haskell`; resource-discipline framing for memory is in `data/ownership` (which covers Linear Haskell among other multiplicity systems).

The lesson is that adding linearity to an existing Core IR is much cheaper than designing a new IR around it: the type system already has the machinery for parameterised types, and multiplicities slot in as another parameter dimension.
